data.title: Parc Bidco AS - Approval of prospectus for listing of senior secured bonds
data.description:
Oslo, 17 February 2026: On 11 February 2026, the Financial SupervisoryAuthorityof Norway approved a prospectus prepared by the company in connection with thelisting of the Parc Bidco AS FRN senior secured EUR 200,000,000 bonds2025/2028(ISIN NO00135478800) on Euronext Oslo Børs. Trading in the bonds commencedtoday, 17 February 2026.

The prospectus and final terms are available on the company's website:https://www.activeon.no/investor-relations

For further information, please contact:

dag@activeon.no

This information is subject to the disclosure requirements pursuant to section5-12 of the Norwegian Securities Trading Act.

https://newsweb.oslobors.no/message/666027

data.title: Sterling Faces Further Underperformance After Weak Jobs Data — Market Talk
data.description: Sterling is the worst-performing G-10 currency after weak U.K. jobs data and this underperformance could extend further unless Wednesday's U.K. inflation figures exceed forecasts, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. Data Tuesday showed the unemployment rate unexpectedly rose to 5.2% and wage growth slowed in the three months to December. This should make Bank of England Governor Andrew Bailey, a key swing voter, more confident about cutting interest rates, Halpenny says. If inflation data for January eases in line with expectations, this will argue for a March rate cut and keep sterling under pressure, he says. Sterling falls to an 11-day low of $1.3549, LSEG data show. The euro rises to a one-week high of 0.8730 pounds. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Heimar hf.: Launch of Share Buy-Back Program
data.description:
At the Annual General Meeting of Heimar hf. (“Heimar” or the “Company”) held on 11 March 2025, shareholders approved an authorisation for the Board of Directors to repurchase up to 10% of the Company’s total issued share capital for the purpose of establishing a trading facility in the Company’s shares and/or implementing a formal share buyback program.

The Board of Directors of Heimar has, based on this mandate issued by the Annual General Meeting, decided on the execution of a share buyback program. The purpose of the program is to decrease the number of issued shares. The amount of the buyback will not exceed ISK 500,000,000 in total purchase price. The number of shares are determined by the purchase price of the shares at the time of execution of the buyback. As of today, Heimar does own 54,523,000 shares.

ACRO verðbréf hf. (“Acro”) will manage the execution of the share buyback program and will make all decisions regarding the acquisition of shares and the timing of purchases independently of Heimar.

The share buyback program will be carried out in accordance with applicable laws and regulations, including the Icelandic Public Limited Companies Act No. 2/1995, Regulation (EU) No. 596/2014 of the European Parliament and of the Council on market abuse (“MAR”), and Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2016/1052 of 8 March 2016, cf. the Icelandic Act on Measures Against Market Abuse No. 60/2021. In making decisions and in the execution of the buyback program, the above provisions shall be complied with, as applicable, and transparency in trading in the Company’s own shares will be ensured.

Under the buyback program, the nominal value of shares purchased on any trading day shall not exceed 25% of the average daily trading volume in Heimar’s shares during January 2026 on Nasdaq Iceland. The purchase price shall not exceed the price of the last independent transaction or the highest current independent bid on Nasdaq Iceland. Pursuant to an agreement between the parties, Acro is authorised to commence repurchases on 17 February 2026. The buyback program will remain in effect until 11 September 2026, or until repurchases for a total consideration of ISK 500,000,000 have been completed, whichever occurs first. Heimar reserves the right to reduce the maximum total consideration to be used by Acro for repurchases or to suspend the buyback program at any time during the program period.

Notices on trading in own shares according to the buyback program will be published in accordance with applicable laws.

Please note that in case of discrepancy in the English and the Icelandic versions, the Icelandic version shall prevail.

For further information please contact:

Björn Eyþór Benediktsson, CFO, eythorb@heimar.is

Attachments

Heimar hf.: Launch of Share Buy-Back Program

data.title: Ryder System: Mispriced as Cyclical, Built to Monetize Control
data.description:
Operating Reality

Strip away the trucks and trailers, and Ryder System is best understood as a capital-backed operating partner. Customers do not come to Ryder primarily to move freight; they come to offload complexity. Fleet ownership, maintenance scheduling, compliance, driver management, and residual value risk are bundled into a service where predictability matters more than headline rates.

The business operates across three activities that reinforce each other.

The first is Fleet Management Solutions. This is the economic core. Ryder owns the vehicles, manages maintenance, handles compliance, and contracts with customers for multi-year terms. For the customer, the decision is rarely about saving money in year one; it is about stabilizing costs, reducing downtime, and avoiding balance-sheet exposure to assets whose value is volatile and regulation-heavy. For Ryder, returns are shaped by asset utilization, maintenance discipline, and residual value realization, not by freight cycles.

The second activity is Dedicated Transportation Solutions. Here Ryder operates customer-specific fleets, often including drivers, routing, and day-to-day execution. These contracts tend to be long-lived because switching providers disrupts operations, labor arrangements, and service quality. Once embedded, Ryder becomes part of the customer's operating system rather than a vendor that can be swapped out easily.

The third is Supply Chain Solutions, encompassing warehousing, distribution, and value-added logistics. This segment is more variable, but it plays a strategic role: it allows Ryder to expand wallet share with existing customers and deepen operational integration. Importantly, supply-chain services are often layered onto fleet relationships, not sold in isolation.

Two structural forces are shaping demand today.

First, outsourcing momentum. Higher interest rates, tighter capital budgets, and increasing regulatory burden have made fleet ownership less attractive for many shippers. The economics increasingly favor operators who can spread capital costs, compliance expertise, and maintenance across a large base. Ryder's scale allows it to absorb volatility that would be punitive for individual operators.

Second, labor and compliance pressure. Driver availability, safety standards, and emissions regulation have all become more complex. For many customers, the risk is no longer whether a truck is available, but whether it can be operated legally and reliably. Handing that responsibility to a specialist with dedicated systems and purchasing power changes the risk profile of the business.

Recent activity reflects this positioning. Ryder has continued to emphasize longer-term contractual relationships and to rebalance its mix toward service-led revenue rather than transactional exposure. Investments in fleet technology, maintenance analytics, and network optimization are not growth initiatives in the conventional sense; they are designed to protect uptime, extend asset life, and reduce variance in outcomes. When the model works, it does not produce dramatic quarter-to-quarter surprises, it produces steady utilization and predictable renewal behavior.

The important point for an owner is this: Ryder's economics are not driven by how many miles are driven in a given quarter. They are driven by how well the company prices control, manages assets over their life, and monetizes reliability for customers who prefer stability to ownership. That distinction explains why the business can look cyclical on the surface while behaving far more steadily underneath.

Financial Spine

Ryder's financial statements read the way you would expect a fleet owner and operator to read: high revenue, modest margins, heavy capital expenditure, and meaningful working-capital movement. If you approach it like a software business, it will look disappointing. If you approach it like a capital-backed service utility, where the product is control and uptime, it starts to make sense.

Start with scale. In its most recent annual report, the company describes itself as a roughly $12.6 billion logistics and transportation platform serving tens of thousands of customers. That revenue base matters because it is diversified across many contracts, not concentrated in a few shippers, and it is supported by a physical network and asset base that new entrants cannot replicate quickly.

The second point is where profitability actually comes from. Ryder's reported revenue includes pass-through and fuel-related items that can obscure the underlying earnings engine. Internally, the business is managed around operating revenue and segment-level profitability, because that is what reflects contract economics rather than commodity inputs. This is one reason the model can look more cyclical in headline revenue than it is in underlying returns.

Capital intensity is the unavoidable reality. Ryder spends billions each year on fleet assets because it owns the equipment that enables the service. That is not a bug; it is the barrier to entry. In the company's latest outlook materials, Ryder indicated annual capital expenditures on the order of ~$2.7 billion, which is a reminder that this is a balance-sheet business first. The economic question is not whether capital spending is high, it is whether the company earns an adequate spread between the cash it generates over an asset's life and the capital it commits upfront.

This is where residual value discipline becomes central. Ryder is constantly recycling assets: acquiring them, operating them inside contracts, and then selling them into the used market. When used vehicle conditions are strong, reported results can look better; when they soften, results can look worse. The important detail for owners is that this variability does not necessarily reflect a change in customer demand. It reflects the timing and pricing of a large secondary-market flow. Investors who treat that noise as the core signal often misread the stability of the contract book underneath.

Cash flow also needs to be read with the asset cycle in mind. Operating cash generation can be substantial, but free cash flow is shaped by fleet growth, replacement needs, and the timing of vehicle purchases versus dispositions. In other words, free cash flow is not a single clean number that stays stable year to year; it is the output of a fleet-reinvestment program. Ryder's own disclosures reflect this by emphasizing cash from operations alongside a defined free-cash-flow measure and detailing how capex and fleet sales move it.

Leverage looks elevated if you view it through a conventional industrial lens, but less so when you recognize the nature of the assets and contracts. Ryder's balance sheet is designed to support long-lived earning assets tied to multi-year customer relationships. The relevant question is not whether debt exists, it will, but whether the company maintains enough flexibility to manage cycles in used vehicle pricing and still fund fleet replacement without diluting the model. The latest outlook commentary continues to emphasize liquidity and the company's targeted balance-sheet posture.

Put simply: Ryder's financial spine is not built for elegance. It is built for repeatable, contract-led returns in a business where control requires capital. If the market prices the company as a purely cyclical transportation name, that is often because it is looking at the wrong line items, headline revenue and used vehicle noise, instead of the durability of the underlying contract economics.

Operating SegmentRevenue (Approx.)% of Total RevenueWhy It Matters for Owners
Fleet Management Solutions (FMS)~$5.89 B~46.6 %Full-service leasing & maintenance; backbone of control-first model
Supply Chain Solutions (SCS)~$5.30 B~41.9 %Integrated logistics and outsourced operations with recurring revenue
Dedicated Transportation Solutions (DTS)~$2.45 B~19.3 %Contracted capacity and reliability; high customer dependency
Total Ryder Revenue (FY2024)~$12.64 B100 %Infrastructure-oriented, contract-heavy revenue mix

Valuation Groundwork

The market usually prices Ryder as if it were a transportation cyclical with a finance arm attached. The more useful comparison, for an owner, is against other ways to get paid for freight-adjacent activity: asset-light logistics, dedicated contract carriers, and integrated operators with meaningful service content.

CompanyBusiness shorthandP/E (TTM)EV/EBITEV/EBITDA
Ryder SystemFleet-backed outsourcing + dedicated16.1614.985.52
JB Hunt TransportIntermodal + contract surface transport33.0223.8414.73
GXO LogisticsAsset-light contract logistics74.4643.5816.31

Two observations matter more than the raw gap.

First, Ryder is priced like an ordinary industrial, despite being a contract-heavy outsourcing platform with long relationships and embedded operations. At roughly mid-teens EV/EBIT, the market is not paying a premium for durability; it is paying a normal multiple for a business that, when executed well, behaves more like a utility for fleet control.

Second, the EV/EBITDA discount is not a trick, it is the balance sheet. Ryder's capital intensity makes EBITDA look large relative to EV. That can be misread as cheap. The correct way to read it is: the company converts capital into contracted operating cash flows, and the market is demanding a conservative price because residual values, working capital, and fleet replacement are real moving parts. In this model, the valuation argument only works if you believe the company can keep earning stable spreads on fleet assets through cycles, not just during favorable used-vehicle conditions.

CompanyBusiness ModelP/E (TTM)EV/EBITDA (TTM)
Ryder SystemFleet & logistics with asset intensity~15.4~5.9
J.B. Hunt Transport ServicesSurface transport & intermodal~32.7~10.7 (LTM)
Sector median (transport/logistics)Broad logistics & transport peers~13.9 median~11.1 median

The following table highlights how Ryder's valuation compares with representative transport and logistics peers across both earnings and enterprise multiples, reinforcing that the market prices the company conservatively relative to asset-light competitors and broader transport averages.

What return is an owner underwriting?

At a mid-teens EV/EBIT and mid-teens P/E, the starting point is a mid-single-digit earnings/EBIT yield. From there, the owner's return is mostly a function of three levers:

  • Inflation-linked pricing and renewal behavior inside multi-year contracts (slow but persistent).
  • Operational improvement (asset utilization, maintenance efficiency, and downtime management).
  • Capital discipline (fleet replacement pacing, residual realization, and not stretching for growth).

If those levers produce something like a steady mid-single-digit earnings base with modest real growth, the math supports a respectable owner return without requiring multiple expansion. If the market ever stops treating Ryder as a pure cycle proxy and instead prices it closer to higher-quality contract logistics peers, the upside comes from rerating, but that is an optionality layer, not the core case.

Trusted Hands

Ryder's shareholder base reinforces the idea that this is a business best understood through capital discipline and operating leverage, not short-term freight trends.

What stands out first is concentration with intent. HG Vora Capital has been one of the most significant long-term owners, with a position that at times represented a meaningful share of its portfolio. While the fund has trimmed recently, the size and duration of its involvement signal familiarity with Ryder's balance-sheet mechanics and a willingness to live through cycle noise to earn asset-level returns. That matters in a company where reported earnings can swing with used-vehicle pricing even as contract economics remain stable.

The presence of Joel Greenblatt (Trades, Portfolio)'s Gotham Asset Management adds a different dimension. Gotham tends to favor businesses where accounting earnings understate normalized economic value. Ryder fits that profile: depreciation timing, residual value realization, and fleet replacement cycles can obscure steady underlying cash generation. Gotham's increased position suggests comfort with those distortions rather than fear of them.

AQR Capital Management and Dimensional Fund Advisors further anchor the register. Both firms typically engage with companies that exhibit persistent factor strength, profitability relative to assets, scale advantages, and structural barriers. In Ryder's case, the factor story is not headline margin expansion but durable spread capture on a very large asset base, supported by long customer relationships and regulatory friction.

The re-entry of Royce Associates is also worth noting. Royce has historically favored asset-heavy franchises where replacement cycles and local scale advantages protect returns. Ryder's national footprint, combined with localized fleet operations, aligns well with that philosophy. This is not a momentum position; it is a comfort-with-structure position.

Finally, the presence of trading-oriented firms like Jane Street and Tudor Investment should not be over-interpreted as conviction signals. Their involvement is more reflective of liquidity, volatility, and capital-markets activity around the stock than a long-term ownership thesis. What matters more is that they coexist alongside owners who are clearly comfortable underwriting multi-year capital cycles.

Taken together, the ownership profile supports the core argument of the article: Ryder attracts investors who understand that value here is created through fleet control, contract duration, and capital recycling, not through timing freight upswings. That kind of shareholder base tends to be patient when the cycle turns, and demanding only when capital discipline slips.

Risks That Matter

Owning Ryder comes with a specific set of risks tied to asset ownership, secondary markets, and contract economics. These are not abstract macro concerns; they are structural features of how the business earns its return.

Residual value exposure is real.

A meaningful share of reported earnings is influenced by the timing and pricing of used-vehicle sales. When secondary markets soften, whether due to OEM overproduction, tighter credit for buyers, or regulatory changes affecting diesel fleets, reported profits can compress even if contract demand remains intact. This does not break the business model, but it can distort near-term results and sentiment. Investors who anchor on quarterly earnings rather than normalized fleet economics may experience volatility that has little to do with customer behavior.

Capital intensity raises the cost of mistakes.

Ryder's moat is built on owning and managing a large fleet. That same feature makes execution errors expensive. Misjudging fleet mix, replacement timing, or customer demand can tie up capital for years. Unlike asset-light peers, Ryder cannot pivot quickly without incurring real economic costs. The company's historical discipline mitigates this risk, but it never disappears in a balance-sheet-led model.

Contract renewal risk is asymmetric.

Dedicated and fleet-management contracts tend to be sticky, but they are not permanent. If a large customer insources operations or switches providers at renewal, Ryder is left with redeploying assets efficiently. The risk is not mass churn, it is lumpy churn. Losing a single large contract can temporarily depress utilization and returns until assets are reabsorbed elsewhere.

Labor availability affects service economics.

While Ryder is less exposed to spot freight cycles, it is deeply exposed to labor markets, drivers, technicians, and warehouse staff. Persistent shortages raise operating costs and can pressure margins if pricing lags wage inflation. The company's scale and training infrastructure help, but labor tightness remains a constraint on profitability rather than demand.

Regulatory shifts can change fleet economics.

Emissions standards, electrification mandates, and safety regulations influence vehicle costs, residual values, and maintenance requirements. Ryder has navigated regulatory change for decades, but transitions, particularly around alternative powertrains, carry execution and capital risks. Early adoption mistakes can depress returns; late adoption can cost contracts.

Leverage magnifies perception risk.

Even when economically rational, leverage makes the equity sensitive to changes in credit conditions. Rising rates increase interest expense and can compress valuation multiples regardless of operating performance. This matters less to the enterprise over time than it does to shareholders in the short run, but it affects how the stock trades.

In short, Ryder's risks stem from owning the system rather than brokering it. The business trades stability of demand for exposure to asset cycles and capital decisions. For long-term owners, the question is not whether these risks exist, they do, but whether the company continues to manage them with discipline. When that discipline holds, the risks tend to create volatility rather than permanent impairment.

Financial Perspective

Stepping back, Ryder's financial profile only looks complicated if you expect clean, linear outcomes. In reality, it behaves exactly like a capital-backed service franchise should.

Over a full cycle, the company converts fleet ownership into contracted cash flows, recycles assets through secondary markets, and redeploys capital into long-duration customer relationships. Earnings volatility mostly reflects timing, used vehicle pricing, replacement cadence, and interest rates, rather than a deterioration in demand or pricing power. That distinction is critical for owners.

From a capital-allocation standpoint, Ryder has become more disciplined over time. Management has leaned into return thresholds on fleet growth, avoided chasing low-quality volume, and treated buybacks opportunistically rather than as a permanent lever. The balance sheet is not optimized for cosmetic ratios; it is structured to ensure liquidity across cycles and flexibility when secondary markets turn.

At today's valuation, the market is not assuming heroic outcomes. Owners are effectively underwriting:

  • Mid-single-digit normalized earnings yields,
  • Low-to-mid single-digit real growth driven by outsourcing penetration and inflation pass-through,
  • And disciplined capital recycling rather than aggressive expansion.

That profile will never screen as cheap on EBITDA alone, nor will it excite growth investors. But for patient capital, the appeal lies in durability without illusion. Ryder does not need freight booms, technology breakthroughs, or multiple expansion to justify ownership. It only needs to keep doing what it has done for decades: control assets better than customers can themselves and price that control rationally. When the market treats the company as a cyclical transport name, it often misses that simple truth. Over time, that gap between perception and operating reality is where long-term returns tend to come from.

data.title: ZAWYA-PRESSR: The Arab Energy Fund closes $346mln reserve-based financing facility to advance development of Iraq’s Block 9
data.description:
Riyadh – The Arab Energy Fund, a leading multilateral impact financial institution, has successfully led and closed a USD 346 million Reserve-Based Financing (RBL) Facility for Kuwait Energy Basra Limited (KEBL), operator of Block 9 (Faihaa Field) in southern Iraq and a wholly owned subsidiary of United Energy Group Limited (UEG), a global integrated energy company listed on the Hong Kong Stock Exchange.

The RBL Facility provides KEBL with support for the next phase of Block 9’s expansion and development. The financing enables continued drilling, infrastructure optimization, and enhanced production potential, ultimately contributing to increased output for Iraq and supporting the country’s ongoing efforts to strengthen its energy sector.

The Arab Energy Fund was mandated in 2024 as the Initial Mandated Lead Arranger and Structuring Bank, leading the transaction from inception through to financial close. Kuwait Finance House B.S.C. (c) (DIFC Branch) and Trafigura Pte. Ltd. joined at the syndication stage as Mandated Lead Arrangers.

Nicolas Thévenot, Chief Banking Officer of The Arab Energy Fund, said: “This successful close reflects The Arab Energy Fund’s ability to structure and lead sophisticated financing solutions grounded in real operational needs, as well as our deep understanding of upstream project fundamentals. We are proud to partner with UEG, KFH, and Trafigura to support the continued expansion of Block 9 and contribute to strengthening Iraq’s energy ecosystem.”

The Faihaa Field represents a key upstream asset for Iraq’s long-term production outlook and a driver of long-term energy security. The Arab Energy Fund remains committed to advancing the sustainable development of national energy sectors across its member states and the wider MENA region.

About The Arab Energy Fund

The Arab Energy Fund (The Fund) is a multilateral impact financial institution focused on the MENA energy and utility sectors, established in 1974 by ten Arab oil-exporting countries. The Fund’s mission is to support the energy ecosystem with debt and equity solutions to enable energy security and sustainability and to develop local value chains and services in the MENA region. The Arab Energy Fund creates impact by contributing to economic prosperity and enabling local communities via talent development and knowledge creation. The Fund offers a comprehensive range of funding solutions across the entire energy value chain to leading public and private sector business partners in over 35 markets. The Arab Energy Fund applies best-practice ESG principles across all operations, with environmental and socially linked projects comprising c.20% of its USD 5.8bn loan portfolio. The Arab Energy Fund is the only energy-focused financial institution in the MENA region rated ‘Aa2’ by Moody’s, ‘AA+’ by Fitch and ‘AA-’ by S&P.

Contacts:

Communications Department

The Arab Energy Fund

Yasa Ahmad

Yasa.Ahmad@edelmansmithfield.com

data.title: U.S. Futures Down, European Stocks Up
data.description:
U.S. futures for the S&P 500 were down 0.2% and futures for the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 0.1%. Changes in futures do not necessarily predict movements after the opening bell.

In Europe, the Stoxx Europe 600 climbed 0.1% in morning trading. Avolta added 3.7% and RELX rose 3.3%. On the other hand, Plus500 dropped 5.9%, and Qiagen lost 3.6%. The FTSE 100 added 0.4%. Other stocks in Europe were mixed as France's CAC 40 increased 0.1% and Germany's DAX fell 0.2%.

The Wall Street Journal Dollar Index was flat at 94.34.

In commodities, Brent crude fell 1% to $67.94 a barrel, and WTI crude fell 1.1% to $63.08 a barrel. The European benchmark price for natural gas, the Dutch futures contract TTF, was down 2.1% to 30.27 euros a megawatt hour.

The German 10-year Bund yield declined by 2 basis points to 2.738% from 2.758%, and the yield on the 10-year U.S. Treasury fell by 2 basis points to 4.029% from 4.052%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions.

Stocks in Asia were down as Japan's Nikkei 225 index declined 0.4%, whereas markets in Hong Kong were closed. Markets in China were closed.

-This article was automatically generated by MarketWatch.

Write to Barcelona Editors at barcelonaeditors@dowjones.com

data.title: BONESUPPORT HOLDING AB (publ) – Invitation to Conference Call and Webcast for Year-End Report 2025
data.description:
BONESUPPORT HOLDING AB (publ), a leading company in orthobiologics for the management of bone injuries, will publish its year-end report Q4 2025, on February 24th at 08.00 CET.

The Company will hold a conference call and an online presentation on the same day at 10.00 CET. The call will be hosted by Torbjörn Sköld, CEO and Håkan Johansson, CFO who will present the results and answer questions. The presentation will be held in English.

Information about logging in to the meeting

If you wish to participate via webcast please use the link below. Via the webcast you are able to ask written questions.

https://bonesupport.events.inderes.com/year-end-report-2025

If you wish to participate via teleconference please register on the link below. After registration you will be provided phone numbers and a conference ID to access the conference. You can ask questions verbally via the teleconference.

https://events.inderes.com/bonesupport/year-end-report-2025/dial-in

For more information contact:

BONESUPPORT Holding AB

Torbjörn Sköld, CEO

+46 (0) 46 286 53 70

Håkan Johansson, CFO

+46 (0) 46 286 53 70

ir@bonesupport.com

Cord Communications

Charlotte Stjerngren

+46 (0) 708 76 87 87

charlotte.stjerngren@cordcom.se

www.cordcom.se

About BONESUPPORT™

BONESUPPORT (Nasdaq Stockholm: BONEX) develops and commercializes innovative injectable bio-ceramic bone graft substitutes that remodel to the patient’s own bone and have the capability of eluting drugs. BONESUPPORT’s bone graft substitutes are based on the patented technology platformCERAMENT. The company is conducting several clinical studies to further demonstrate the clinical and health economic benefits its products deliver. The company is based in Lund, Sweden, and the net sales amounted to SEK 899 million in 2024. Please visitwww.bonesupport.comfor more information.

BONESUPPORT and CERAMENT areregistered trademarksof BONESUPPORT AB.

Attachments

BONESUPPORT HOLDING AB (publ) – Invitation to Conference Call and Webcast for Year-End Report 2025

data.title: ZAWYA-PRESSR: General Motors Africa & Middle East delivers record sales growth of 33% and sweeps prestigious industry awards
data.description:
Dubai, UAE – General Motors (GM) Africa & Middle East closed 2025 with strong momentum as it enters its 100th anniversary in the region, delivering 33% sales growth compared to 2024, driven by demand in key markets by its award-winning Chevrolet, GMC and Cadillac lineup, alongside global recognition for vehicle excellence.

In the Middle East, GM recorded 7% year-over-year growth, led by its flagship pick-up trucks Chevrolet Silverado and GMC Sierra in Iraq, Saudi Arabia, Lebanon, UAE and Bahrain. Across GM’s full-size SUV lineup - Chevrolet Tahoe and Suburban, GMC Yukon and Yukon XL, and Cadillac Escalade and Escalade ESV, sales grew by 7%, with double-digit gains in Kuwait, UAE, Lebanon, Bahrain, and Qatar. Meanwhile, GM Egypt posted a record‑breaking 154% sales performance in 2025 compared to 2024, underpinned by its milestone launch of the locally produced 2025 all‑new Chevrolet Optra and supported by the market’s economic recovery, while Chevrolet continuing to lead the market.

This successful business performance is further underscored by major global awards for GM’s 2026 vehicle lineup.

Honored models include Cadillac CT4-V Blackwing and CT5-V Blackwing by Car and Driver’s 10Best Cars, where the models were praised for precision engineering, manual transmission availability, and supercharged performance.  The 2026 Chevrolet Corvette earned a spot on Car and Driver’s 10Best Car’s list, including the Stingray with the Z51 package and all‑wheel‑drive E‑Ray. Editors highlighted the Stingray coupe with the Z51 package for reaching 0–100 km/h in 3 seconds and the E‑Ray for a 0–100 km/h time as low as 2.6 seconds, praising both for their sharp handling, strong grip, and refined ride. On 10Best Trucks & SUVs, Chevrolet Tahoe and Suburban, and GMC Yukon and Yukon XL were commended for comfort, agility, and towing capacity up to 3,810 kg.

Adding to the accolades, TopGear.com awarded GM three titles in its inaugural U.S. Car Awards: Chevrolet Corvette ZR1 – U.S. Supercar of the Year, Cadillac LYRIQ-V – U.S. SUV of the Year, and Cadillac – U.S. Manufacturer of the Year. The ZR1 impressed with its twin-turbo 5.5L V8 producing up to 1,064 horsepower. and Nürburgring lap records, while LYRIQ-V, Cadillac’s first EV with a “V” badge, delivered 0–100 km/h in 3.4 seconds with Velocity Max.

Commenting on the results, Rohan Fernandes, Managing Director of Commercial Operations at GM Africa & Middle East, said: “2025 was a defining year for General Motors in the region because our customers and dealer partners made it one. Their passion for our capable pickups and iconic full‑size SUVs continues to power our growth across the Middle East. As we continue to offer a wide range of vehicles that fit every price point across all propulsions, paired with our pioneering in‑vehicle connected technology experience enabled by OnStar, we’re reshaping mobility in the region. Our momentum is clearly recognized by global awards from Car and Driver and TopGear.com, reinforcing our commitment to engineering excellence, innovation and customer value.”

Fernandes added, “At GM Africa and Middle East, we are proud to be celebrating our centenary in the region – 100 years of bringing customers world-class vehicles and leading technology that they have come to love and trust under our Chevrolet, GMC and Cadillac brands. We are embarking on our next 100 years with exciting, new products and innovations – such as the world’s first true hands-free driving technology, Super Cruise* 2026 will be a landmark year.”

* Super Cruise features may vary by country and are subject to local regulatory approvals.

About General Motors Africa & Middle East Operations

General Motors is driving the future of transportation, leveraging advanced technology to build safer, smarter, and lower emission cars, trucks, and SUVs. GM’s Buick, Cadillac, Chevrolet, and GMC brands offer a broad portfolio of innovative gasoline-powered vehicles and the industry’s widest range of EVs, as we move to an all-electric future.

The GM Africa & Middle East operations have been in the region for close to 100 years, now headquartered in Dubai, UAE with a manufacturing plant in Egypt. In the region, GM represents Chevrolet, GMC and Cadillac. With a network of 17 distributors serving 29 countries, the organization has over 203 customer-facing rooftops to cater to countries in Africa, Levant, the GCC and other Middle Eastern countries. For more information, please visit www.gmarabia.com.    

CONTACT:

Diana Al Shafie

Corporate Communications Lead

GM Africa & Middle East

Email: diana.alshafie@gm.com

Send us your press releases to pressrelease.zawya@lseg.com

data.title: Loss-making DeFi lender ZeroLend to shut down after 98% TVL drop
data.description:
ZeroLend, a decentralized lending protocol that once held nearly $359 million in user deposits, is shutting down. Founder "Ryker" announced the wind-down on Monday in a post on X, citing persistent operational losses and a deteriorating ecosystem that made the protocol unsustainable.

The protocol's total value locked has cratered 98% from its November 2024 peak to just $6.6 million as of Monday, according to DefiLlama data. The remaining TVL is currently distributed across Linea ($2.75 million), Ethereum ($1.97 million), and ZKsync Era ($933,000), with smaller balances remaining on Blast, Sonic, and Base, among other networks. 

The collapse follows a prolonged period during which ZeroLend operated at a loss, with the founder pointing to several factors, including multiple early-stage chains becoming inactive or illiquid, oracle providers discontinuing support, and increased attention from hackers and scammers as the protocol grew.

DefiLlama data shows the protocol's earnings deteriorating sharply. After generating $205,990 during 2024, ZeroLend's earnings rose to $1.17 million in 2025 before falling to $132,290 in the current year-to-date period. Gross protocol revenue followed a similar trajectory, peaking at $3.11 million in 2025 before dropping to around $355,000 in 2026. The protocol has never distributed income to token holders.

What’s next? 

"Our immediate priority is ensuring users can safely withdraw their assets," Ryker wrote in the post. "Most markets have already been set to 0% LTV, and we strongly encourage all users to withdraw any remaining funds from the platform."

For assets residing on Manta, Zircuit, and xLayer, where DefiLlama showed ZeroLend pools at around $76,000 and $13,000 for Manta and xLayer, respectively, the team is developing a recovery solution, according to Ryker. This involves a planned "timelock upgrade" to the protocol’s smart contracts intended to redistribute funds currently locked in these “illiquid” or inactive environments.

Regarding LBTC users on Base, Ryker addressed a separate incident from February 2024. The team has been working to trace and recover those funds and will provide partial refunds using LINEA airdrop allocations received by the team. Affected users have been instructed to contact moderators or submit support tickets.

For ZERO token holders, Ryker offered no recovery path. "For token holders, this marks the conclusion of the ZeroLend journey," he wrote.

The ZERO token has fallen 34% over the past 24 hours and has lost nearly all its value since hitting a peak of $0.001 in May 2024, according to CoinGecko.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

© 2026 The Block. All Rights Reserved. This article is provided for informational purposes only. It is not offered or intended to be used as legal, tax, investment, financial, or other advice.

data.title: Kalyan Jewellers promoter buys additional 4.65 lakh shares
data.description:
Shares of Kalyan Jewellers India Ltd. were trading with losses on Tuesday as its promoter bought additional stake in the firm.

The company's promoter has bought an additional 4.65 lakh shares in Kalyan Jewellers. Since February 9, the promoter has bought 9.25 lakh shares in the firm.

Kalyan Jewellers reported its third quarter earnings earlier this month.

Its revenue increased 42.1% to ₹10,343.4 crore from ₹7,278 crore in the previous year. Its profit after tax surged 90.4% to ₹416.2 crore from 218.6 crore in the third quarter of the previous fiscal.

The company's EBITDA increased 74.6% to ₹750.4 crore from ₹429.9 crore last year and its EBITDA margins expanded to 7.3% from 5.9% in the year-ago period.

Its same store sales growth stood at 27% while the share of studded jewellery improved to 31.2% from 29.5% in the previous year.

The non-south markets continued to drive growth, contributing 58.5% of the total revenue in the third quarter.

The jewellery firm said margin expansion was supported by a favourable mix, operating leverage and gains in categroies such as silver and platinum.

Shares of Kalyan Jewellers India were trading 0.9% lower at ₹423.4 apiece around 2.10 pm on Tuesday. The stock has declined 19% in the past six months.

data.title: FTSE MIB Drifts Higher
data.description:
The Italian FTSE MIB rose 0.2% to around 45,500 in early trading on Tuesday, taking a breather after five consecutive subdued sessions and moving in line with most of its European counterparts.

Gains were led by heavyweight financials, including UniCredit (+0.9%), Intesa Sanpaolo (+0.8%), Assicurazioni Generali (+0.7%), and Mediobanca (+1.2%).

Other notable gainers included Enel (+1.0%), Snam (+0.6%), and Italgas (+0.7%).

On the flip side, gains were capped by tech-related shares, weighed down by lingering concerns over AI disruptions, with Prysmian and STMicroelectronics down 2.7% and 1.3%, respectively.

Defence stock Leonardo SpA (-1.8%) and industrial firm Buzzi Unicem (-1.4%) were also in the red.

On the economic front, investors now await Italy’s December trade balance figures, with the surplus expected to narrow.
